topic: I Built an AI-Powered System to Research 162 Companies in a Day — Here's What I Learned
title: I Built an AI-Powered System to Research 162 Companies in a Day
subtitle: How a growth engineer used Claude Code, browser automation, and a Tinder-style swipe tool to turn job searching into a data problem — and what 162 European startups revealed about the hiring landscape
summary: Job searching as a senior engineer is broken. You're either swiping LinkedIn blind or spray-and-praying CVs into the void. I built an AI-powered research pipeline that evaluated 162 companies in a single day — complete with reputation scoring, automated screenshots, and a custom swipe tool. Along the way, I discovered that Glassdoor scores are systematically misleading, that the best companies for my skills aren't the ones I find most exciting, and that the entire build-vs-buy equation has flipped when you have an AI pair programmer.
review_notes: All quality gates passed. 44+ external links (target 13), 20+ real-world examples (target 4), 100% prose ratio (target 75%). Expanded from 3,068 initial draft through two revision passes to 5,000, then major expansion adding Section 5 (outreach machine) and Puppeteer narrative update to 6,452.

narrative_arc.hook: Every other candidate sends a CV. What if you showed up having already done the work?
narrative_arc.tension: 162 companies. No signal. How do you find the right one when job boards show you everything except what matters?
narrative_arc.resolution: Build the research tool yourself — AI agents, reputation scoring, a swipe interface, and automated screenshots
narrative_arc.transformation: Reader understands how to treat job searching as an engineering problem, not a lottery

sections.title: The Recruiter's Inbox Problem

scaffold.opening_hook: I've shipped three products to exit, led teams from zero to a million users, and been featured in an MIT course taken by 50,000 students. And I still can't figure out where to apply for a job.
scaffold.central_argument: Job searching for senior engineers is fundamentally broken — the tools are designed for volume, not signal
key_questions: Why do senior engineers with strong track records still struggle with job searching?
What's wrong with LinkedIn/Indeed/Wellfound for someone who knows exactly what they want?
What would it look like to treat this as an engineering problem?
scaffold.closing_bridge: So I did what any engineer would do — I built something

research_questions: What are current stats on senior engineer job search frustration?
How many applications does the average senior engineer send before landing a role?
What do hiring managers say about the quality of inbound applications?

sections.title: What I Was Actually Looking For

scaffold.opening_hook: Prague, February 2026. I'm a full-stack engineer with 15 years of experience, three exits, and a very specific problem.
scaffold.central_argument: The more experienced you are, the harder it is to find the right fit — because 'right' stops meaning 'any job that pays' and starts meaning something much more specific
key_questions: What does a senior engineer with 3 exits actually want from their next role?
Why is the European startup landscape harder to navigate than US/global?
What makes this search different from a typical job hunt?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The criteria were clear. The problem was finding 160+ companies that might match — without spending three months on LinkedIn

research_questions: What is the European startup landscape like for senior engineers in 2025-2026?
How do experienced engineers define 'fit' beyond salary and title?
What verticals are growing in European tech?

sections.title: Swiping Blind on 162 Companies

scaffold.opening_hook: The first research sprint produced 101 companies in a few hours. And they were almost useless.
scaffold.central_argument: Scale without context creates a new problem — you have data but no signal. The swipe tool exposed that you can't make decisions from one-line descriptions
key_questions: What happens when you automate research but not understanding?
How do you make yes/no decisions on companies you've never heard of?
What data do you actually need to evaluate a company?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The tool was fast. The data feeding it wasn't good enough. Time to build the machine properly.

research_questions: What are common pitfalls of automated company research?
How do job seekers typically evaluate companies beyond job boards?
What signals matter most when evaluating startup quality?

sections.title: Building the Machine

scaffold.opening_hook: The system has five layers, and each one exists because the previous one broke.
scaffold.central_argument: Each component was built reactively — not designed upfront, but created when a real problem emerged. The AI agents did research. The swipe tool exposed data gaps. The reputation scoring caught hidden landmines. The screenshot service replaced two broken SaaS products.
key_questions: What does an AI-powered company research pipeline actually look like?
How do you build a reputation scoring system for companies?
When does 'just build it' beat using a SaaS tool?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The machine was running. 162 companies scored, rated, and ready to review. But the most interesting findings weren't in the data — they were in the patterns.

research_questions: What tools exist for automated company research?
How accurate are Glassdoor reviews — research and analysis?
What is the current state of Puppeteer for browser automation?
How are AI agents being used for research tasks in 2025-2026?
What is Claude Code and how does it work for agentic workflows?

sections.title: What 162 Companies Actually Taught Me

scaffold.opening_hook: The data told three stories I wasn't expecting.
scaffold.central_argument: Three counterintuitive findings: (1) Glassdoor scores are systematically misleading — high ratings hide mass layoffs, (2) The most exciting companies don't need your skills, the boring-sounding ones do, (3) Agencies solve the 'can't pick one vertical' problem
key_questions: How specifically are Glassdoor scores misleading?
What's the tension between exciting domains and practical skill fit?
Why did the agency angle emerge as a strategic breakthrough?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The system didn't just find companies — it changed how I think about what I'm looking for

research_questions: What research exists on Glassdoor review manipulation or inaccuracy?
How do agencies like ustwo, Bakken & Baeck position their engineering teams?
What is the 'acquire-and-fire' pattern in tech M&A?

sections.title: Growth Engineering Your Own Career

scaffold.opening_hook: The irony isn't lost on me: I used growth engineering to find a growth engineering job.
scaffold.central_argument: The meta-lesson: the tools and thinking you use in your day job are the same ones that make you stand out in a job search. The build IS the portfolio. The process IS the pitch.
key_questions: What can other engineers learn from this approach?
How does 'demonstrate, don't apply' change the hiring dynamic?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The system found me 162 companies. But the real product wasn't the database — it was the proof that I know how to build things that find signal in noise.

research_questions: What outreach strategies work for senior engineers beyond standard applications?
How do growth engineers typically demonstrate value to prospective employers?
What's the current discourse on 'building in public' as a career strategy?

sources.title: State of the software engineering jobs market, 2025: what hiring managers see
sources.author: Gergely Orosz

metadata.target_audience: Senior software engineers frustrated with traditional job searching, growth engineers, developers interested in AI-assisted workflows, HN/Dev.to readers who appreciate technical deep-dives on unconventional approaches
